content:
> How does it know to not render the inner part at all while still doing multiple entirely separate frames with their own distinct animation phases and so on for the outer part?

Because from the framework layer's perspective, it is still one single big frame, running the slow single build/layout phase. flutter_smooth, during "preemption", is using the layer tree of *last* frame (thus it contains only original page, and the new page is not yet there) with modifications (e.g. shift 50px).
Then how does anything animate during the one big frame?

> Then how does anything animate during the one big frame?

Effectively, it is done by modifying only that part of layer tree at low level, and the high-level thing is that, the part you want to animate has its build/layout called during preemption.
can you elaborate? for example, are we calling transient callbacks in the scheduler?

Sure! IMHO we do not call those callbacks (because otherwise the semantics may be confusing). However, we do let the smooth subtree do extra build/layout, and let Tickers do extra ticks (with timestamps of preemption times) - that will make animations animate. This seems to work well for e.g. page animations (see example), list view scrolling (see example), shimmers (since it is leaf widget), etc.

P.S. The majority of user code will be unaware of these things IMHO. Firstly, most user code will be in the "janky" part (e.g. the complex new page in the "page transition" example), and thus the code will not feel anything different and flutter_smooth will not touch it at all. Secondly, it is the "page animation widget" itself that will need to work with flutter_smooth. However, it is common to encapsulate it into reusable packages, and thus users only need to import and use it, still without knowing flutter_smooth. Thirdly, if users write down animating widgets by themselves, the changed API (e.g. extra ticks) seems to either not require user to change anything, or to change minimally. For example, suppose users write down an AnimatedBuilder, then the code usually does not care how many times `build` is called or ticker is ticked.

Tickers tick by sending a message as a transient callback. I don't understand what you mean by letting tickers tick without doing the transient callbacks.

They also animate by marking widgets dirty. If we are just continuing the build phase where we interrupted, how do we go back and re-update the widgets that we have already built to make the animation work?

> P.S. The majority of user code will be unaware of these things

That may be the case (though in general I think that's a bad thing; we should design our framework so that it is transparent and people can understand it). But it's irrelevant to the problem of getting the people who are reviewing the proposal to understand it. We have to fully understand it before we can evaluate it.

> They also animate by marking widgets dirty. If we are just continuing the build phase where we interrupted, how do we go back and re-update the widgets that we have already built to make the animation work?

I guess this page https://cjycode.com/flutter_smooth/design/infra/preempt/what-high explains it. Feel free to ping me if need more info!

> Tickers tick by sending a message as a transient callback. I don't understand what you mean by letting tickers tick without doing the transient callbacks.

It seems that we can do it by directly calling tick (https://github.com/fzyzcjy/flutter_smooth/blob/f6ffffe960468316a01191b4fd6b18c83a04e3cc/packages/smooth/lib/src/infra/auxiliary_tree_pack.dart#L103 & https://github.com/fzyzcjy/flutter/blob/17e38b91d20317a6a56c042b9849dd466c9e5fac/packages/flutter/lib/src/scheduler/ticker.dart#L265).

> But it's irrelevant to the problem of getting the people who are reviewing the proposal to understand it. We have to fully understand it before we can evaluate it.

Definitely! I am just adding this "P.S." to say that it may not make end users change *their* code a lot, i.e. developer experience seems OK, instead of talking about Flutter framework/engine reviewers.
I don't understand that first link. We're creating multiple widget trees?

> We're creating multiple widget trees?

IMHO yes. We are having extra BuildOwner/PipelineOwner/... and runs extra buildScope/flushLayout/... for the parts that needs to be smooth (code e.g.: https://github.com/fzyzcjy/flutter_smooth/blob/f6ffffe960468316a01191b4fd6b18c83a04e3cc/packages/smooth/lib/src/infra/auxiliary_tree_pack.dart#L107-L121)

i see. so basically your proposal is to split the widget tree and render tree into multiple subtrees that we can pump independently, merging their layer trees when updating the engine's layer tree, and when one subtree takes too long to build or layout, you just don't update it in the main layer tree?

why change the engine for this? it seems like it would be possible to pump the outer tree normally, with the inner trees getting getting interrupted when they are pumped if they go over budget?

> so basically your proposal is to split the widget tree and render tree into multiple subtrees that we can pump independently, merging their layer trees when updating the engine's layer tree, and when one subtree takes too long to build or layout, you just don't update it in the main layer tree?

I guess it is roughly like that. Just it is not very like "independent" things, since the smooth tree does not have real transient callbacks etc like you have mentioned.

> why change the engine for this? it seems like it would be possible to pump the outer tree normally, with the inner trees getting getting interrupted when they are pumped if they go over budget?

For example, one change to engine is that, allow to call `window.render` multiple times inside one plain-old frame. You know, one time for the classical code, and zero to multiple extra times for the preemption. Last time (when developing flutter_smooth) when I checked it, it seems that only one render call is allowed.
but why not just do a single window.render per frame? is it just because of the difficulty with unwinding the stack so you can resume layouts?

> but why not just do a single window.render per frame? is it just because of the difficulty with unwinding the stack so you can resume layouts?

I think that is one major reason. Depending how we unwind the stack, some more details are shown in https://cjycode.com/flutter_smooth/design/review/comparison.

Hi @Hixie,

Thanks so much for reviewing this proposal and for your insightful questions – they've definitely helped bring more clarity to the discussion.

We understand this is a complex proposal with implications across multiple layers of the Flutter engine. To help us all understand the path forward (or if there isn't one at this time), we'd be grateful if you could share your perspective on:

1.  Are there specific blockers or unresolved concerns that are currently preventing this proposal from progressing?
2.  Do you foresee any significant shortcomings, potential pitfalls, or architectural challenges with the approach as outlined?

@fzyzcjy has been incredibly diligent in providing details and examples, and I'm sure they'd be happy to elaborate further or answer any more specific questions you might have.

Many in the community, myself included, are keen to see this area explored. If there are ways we can help address any identified concerns or bridge gaps in the proposal, please let us know. We're eager to contribute where possible.

Thanks again for your engagement on this!

As discussed above, I don't feel like I sufficiently understand the trade-offs of this proposal. My recommendation would be for anyone who wants to progress here to actually implement it, and demonstrate how it works, and why it's a good idea. Benchmarks showing impact on existing apps would be especially useful, as would benchmarks showing how it improves matters for apps making use of this feature.

That said, to be honest, based on what I've seen so far, I don't really think this is the right solution to any problem. It's adding a lot of complexity when the solution to performance problems during layout is the opposite: it's removing complexity. You make things fast by doing less, not more.

I haven't see test cases showing performance problems during build and layout where the problems aren't solvable by writing better code (e.g. removing expensive code from widget build methods). When people say their app is slow, they rarely follow-up by posting test cases that would let us figure out why. As there are plenty of people writing fast apps, it's unlikely, IMHO, that the problem is with the framework. But if it is, the only way that's going to get fixed is to provide test cases showing that.

> the solution to performance problems during layout is removing complexity, doing less, not more.

## Framework can receive TextEditingDeltas from engine

## Description
[Design Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PXNd_LwJudiUTnCM2AXRxbd98_p4BlFf6qFhEhv3wvs/edit?usp=sharing)

This change implements a new channel for the `TextInputClient`. 

`TextInputClient.updateEditingValueWithDeltas(List<TextEditingDelta> deltas)` will be used to receive changes made to the editing value from the engine. 

This delta model is `opt-in`able through a flag in `TextInputConfiguration` called `enableDeltaModel`. This will tell the engine to use `updateEditingValueWithDeltas` instead of `updateEditingValue`.

---|---

final TextEditingController _controller = TextEditingController();

          children: <Widget>[
            TextField(
              autofocus: true,
